mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List
notes: Cisco's 'show interfaces status' exists on switching platforms only; on routers use 'show ip interface brief' instead.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List > select port

notes: DOM data only appears on optics that support digital monitoring; on Ruckus FastIron enable 'optical-monitor' first.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration (Description field)
notes: AOS-S uses 'name' and Ruckus FastIron uses 'port-name' instead of 'description'.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Modify Port Configuration (assign 'disabled' profile)
notes: AOS-S and Ruckus use 'disable' in the interface context rather than 'shutdown'.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Modify Port Configuration (remove 'disabled' profile)
notes: Junos has no 'no shutdown' equivalent — you re-enable by deleting the disable statement, while AOS-S and Ruckus use 'enable'.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List > select port for traffic insights
notes: Cisco 'show interfaces <port> counters' is Catalyst switch syntax — on IOS routers use plain 'show interfaces <port>' for packet/byte counts.
On AOS-CX this zeroes counters only for your current CLI session — use 'clear interface statistics global' to reset them for all sessions.

commands.junos:
set interfaces {{interface}} ether-options speed 1g
set interfaces {{interface}} ether-options link-mode full-duplex

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration > edit port profile (Speed/Duplex)
notes: The Junos hierarchy varies by EX/QFX generation — newer platforms put forced speed and autoneg under 'ether-options' (e.g. 'set interfaces ge-0/0/0 ether-options no-auto-negotiation') — so confirm on your platform before pasting.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: MTU is set in the port profile under Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration
notes: AOS-S has no per-port MTU (jumbo is enabled per VLAN), Ruckus 'jumbo' is global and needs write memory plus a reload, and per-port mtu on Catalyst requires IOS XE 17.1+ (older Catalyst L2 switches use global 'system mtu jumbo 9000' plus a reload instead).

task: Configure a range of ports at once

commands.junos:
set interfaces interface-range RANGE1 member-range ge-0/0/0 to ge-0/0/3
set interfaces interface-range RANGE1 disable

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration accepts port ranges (e.g. ge-0/0/0-3)
notes: Junos has no interactive range context — the interface-range group (named RANGE1 here) is committed configuration that persists, unlike the throwaway range contexts on IOS-style CLIs.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List
notes: AOS-S calls port descriptions 'names' (hence 'show name'), and on AOS-CX and Ruckus the description/name is the right-most column of the brief listing and may be truncated.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List
notes: AOS-S uses the plural 'show vlans' while AOS-CX and Ruckus use the singular 'show vlan'.

task: Create a VLAN with a name
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > Shared Elements > Networks
notes: Junos keys the VLAN by its name (USERS) with the numeric ID as an attribute — the reverse of IOS-style CLIs.

task: Assign access (untagged) port to a VLAN

commands.junos:
set interfaces {{interface}} unit 0 family ethernet-switching interface-mode access
set interfaces {{interface}} unit 0 family ethernet-switching vlan members {{vlan}}

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration (assign an access port profile)
notes: AOS-S and Ruckus FastIron assign untagged membership from the VLAN context, not the interface context.

task: Configure trunk/tagged VLAN on a port

commands.junos:
set interfaces {{interface}} unit 0 family ethernet-switching interface-mode trunk
set interfaces {{interface}} unit 0 family ethernet-switching vlan members {{vlan}}

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration (assign a trunk port profile)
notes: On Cisco IOS, 'switchport trunk allowed vlan <id>' replaces the existing allowed list — use 'switchport trunk allowed vlan add <id>' to append to a live trunk.

task: Show which ports are in a VLAN
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List (VLAN column)
notes: Junos identifies VLANs by configured name rather than numeric ID, so pass the VLAN name (e.g. vlan-100) to show vlans.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > Shared Elements > Networks (delete the network)
notes: On Cisco IOS, access ports still assigned to a deleted VLAN go inactive and silently blackhole traffic until reassigned, and Junos deletes by VLAN name with delete vlans <name>.

task: Set native/untagged VLAN on a trunk port

commands.junos:
set interfaces {{interface}} native-vlan-id {{vlan}}
set interfaces {{interface}} unit 0 family ethernet-switching vlan members {{vlan}}

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > Port Profiles (trunk mode, Port Network = native VLAN)
notes: AOS-S and Ruckus have no per-port native-vlan keyword (untagged membership is set from VLAN context, and Ruckus releases before FastIron 08.0.80 instead need the port tagged plus dual-mode <vlan-id> under the interface), while Junos also requires the native VLAN in the trunk's vlan members list.

task: Configure voice VLAN on a port
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > Port Profiles (set VoIP Network on the profile)
notes: Phones learn the voice VLAN via LLDP-MED on all five vendors, and on Junos the value must be the configured VLAN name (not the numeric ID).

task: Create an SVI (VLAN interface) with an IP

commands.junos:
set vlans {{vlan}} l3-interface irb.{{vlan}}
set interfaces irb unit {{vlan}} family inet address {{ip}}/24

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Other IP Configuration (add an IP on the network)
notes: Ruckus needs the Layer 3 (router) FastIron image for ve interfaces, and on Junos the irb unit replaces the older interface vlan RVI used by pre-ELS EX code.

task: Enable IGMP snooping on a VLAN
mist_note: Mist dashboard: no IGMP Snooping section in the UI — add 'set protocols igmp-snooping vlan all' under Organization > Switch Templates > Additional CLI Commands
notes: Cisco IOS already has IGMP snooping on by default for every VLAN, and on Ruckus choosing multicast active instead of passive also makes the switch the IGMP querier.
Junos drops the "ip" keyword — the command is "show route", not "show ip route".
AOS-CX uses the plural "neighbors" while AOS-S, Cisco IOS, and Ruckus FastIron use the singular "neighbor".
AOS-S 2530/2930F-class switches do not support BGP, so no aos-s equivalent exists.
AOS-CX and Junos take CIDR prefix notation while AOS-S and Cisco IOS require a dotted-decimal mask (FastIron accepts either form).

task: Look up the route to a destination
notes: Junos omits the 'ip' keyword — 'show ip route' is not a valid command on EX/QFX.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Static Route (also at Organization > Switch Templates)
notes: On L2-only AOS-S models such as the 2530, IP routing is unavailable — use 'ip default-gateway <ip>' instead of an ip route statement.
AOS-S has no 'brief' form — 'show ip' prints the per-VLAN IP table, and Junos 'show interfaces terse' includes L2 ports too (filter with '| match irb' for SVIs only).

task: Configure DHCP relay (IP helper) on an SVI
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Services > DHCP Server / Relay
notes: On non-ELS Junos EX the SVI is vlan.<unit> rather than irb.<unit>, and the FastIron ve number matches the VLAN id only by convention (it is whatever 'router-interface ve' assigned under the VLAN).

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] (firmware version in switch details)
notes: On AOS-S, show version reports only the software revision — use show system for serial number and uptime, which Cisco IOS includes in show version.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Download Junos Config
notes: Junos has no show running-config — use show configuration from operational mode (append | display set for set-style output).
Junos has no separate save step — commit is run from configuration mode and both applies and persists the candidate configuration.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Reboot Switch
notes: AOS-CX has no reload command — boot system is the reboot, and it prompts to save if there are unsaved configuration changes.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > rename switch (pushed to the device as its Junos host-name)
notes: Junos spells it host-name (hyphenated) under the system hierarchy, not hostname.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Switch Insights (CPU & Memory Utilization)
notes: Cisco IOS and FastIron report memory with a separate command (show processes memory / show memory) — the commands shown cover CPU only on those two platforms.
Aruba splits environment status across commands — AOS-CX also has show environment fan and show environment power-supply, and AOS-S also has show system temperature and show system power-supply.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Inventory
notes: FastIron has no show inventory command — serial numbers appear in show version output.

task: Back up the config to a TFTP server
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Download Junos Config
notes: Junos cannot copy files over TFTP (use file copy /config/juniper.conf.gz scp://<server>/backup.cfg instead), and AOS-CX requires the trailing cli format keyword.

commands.aos-s:
timesync ntp
ntp unicast
ntp server {{ip}}
ntp enable

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > [template] > All Switches Configuration > NTP
notes: Older AOS-S firmware (2530 class) may only support SNTP (timesync sntp / sntp unicast / sntp server priority 1 <ip>), and FastIron will not sync until the clock is first set within 1000 seconds of real time (clock set).

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > [template] > All Switches Configuration > DNS

task: Send logs to a syslog server

commands.junos: set system syslog host {{ip}} any any

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > [template] > All Switches Configuration > Syslog
notes: The Junos line logs every facility at every severity (any any) — most deployments tighten this to any notice or any warning.

commands.aos-cx: user netadmin group administrators password plaintext PASSWORD
commands.aos-s: password manager user-name netadmin plaintext PASSWORD
commands.junos: set system login user netadmin class super-user authentication plain-text-password

notes: Junos prompts interactively for the password, AOS-S has no arbitrary local users (only the fixed manager/operator accounts), and FastIron local users only take effect at login once aaa authentication login default local is configured.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Testing Tools > Ping
notes: Junos ping runs continuously until Ctrl+C, so a count is included here; the other vendors stop after a fixed packet count by default.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Testing Tools > Traceroute
Mist dashboard: Monitor > Service Levels > Insights > [switch] > Switch Events
notes: AOS-CX prints oldest entries first (add '-r' for newest first), and Junos reads a named syslog file rather than a 'show logging' buffer.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Clients > Wired Clients
notes: Older Cisco IOS releases use the hyphenated 'show mac-address-table', while AOS-S and Ruckus drop 'table' from the command entirely.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List
notes: Cisco IOS ships with LLDP disabled (enable with 'lldp run') and CDP enabled, so 'show cdp neighbors' often works when LLDP returns nothing.

task: Ping with a source interface or VLAN
notes: Junos sources from the interface you name (the SVI is irb.<unit> on ELS switches, vlan.<unit> on older EX), while Ruckus FastIron only accepts a source IP address, so replace 10.0.0.1 with the VE's own address.

task: Find which port a MAC address is on
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Clients > Wired Clients (search the MAC)
notes: MAC notation differs by vendor: Cisco and Ruckus use 001b.2c3d.4e5f, AOS-CX and Junos use 00:1b:2c:3d:4e:5f, and AOS-S uses 001b2c-3d4e5f.

task: Show MAC addresses learned on a port
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List

notes: On AOS-CX a bare clear arp only flushes the default VRF; add vrf all-vrfs to clear every VRF.

task: Mirror a port to a sniffer port (SPAN)

commands.aos-cx:
mirror session 1
destination interface 1/1/48
source interface {{interface}} both
enable
commands.junos:
set forwarding-options analyzer SPAN input ingress interface {{interface}}.0
set forwarding-options analyzer SPAN input egress interface {{interface}}.0
set forwarding-options analyzer SPAN output interface ge-0/0/47.0
commands.cisco-ios:
monitor session 1 source interface {{interface}} both
monitor session 1 destination interface GigabitEthernet1/0/48

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Mirroring
notes: Mirror destination ports stop forwarding normal traffic on most platforms, and pre-ELS Junos (older EX) configures the analyzer under ethernet-switching-options instead of forwarding-options.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Cable Test
notes: TDR works only on copper ports and briefly drops the link, and results are read with a second command on most platforms (show cable-diagnostics tdr on Cisco/Ruckus, diag cable-diagnostic show <port> on AOS-CX, show diagnostics tdr interface <port> on Junos).

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Utilities > Send Switch Log to Mist
notes: Output runs to thousands of lines, so enable terminal session logging or redirect to a file before running it.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List (per-port STP state/role)
notes: Ruckus FastIron's display command depends on the STP flavor in use — 'show span' for per-VLAN 802.1D, 'show 802-1w' for RSTP, 'show mstp' for MSTP; on Junos add 'show spanning-tree interface' for per-port roles/states.

Only Cisco IOS and AOS-CX have a dedicated root summary command (AOS-CX supports it in both MSTP and RPVST+ modes); AOS-S, Junos, and Ruckus report the root bridge ID, cost, and root port inside their general spanning-tree output.

task: Make a port an edge port (PortFast)
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration > assign a port profile with 'STP Edge' enabled
notes: AOS-S and Ruckus take the port inline at global config (no interface context), and the Ruckus form assumes 802.1W is enabled globally — with per-VLAN RSTP enter the same line under the VLAN context (e.g. vlan 10) instead.

task: Enable BPDU guard on a port

commands.junos:
set protocols rstp interface {{interface}} edge
set protocols rstp bpdu-block-on-edge

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration > port profile with 'STP Edge' enabled (BPDU guard is applied automatically)
notes: Junos has no per-port BPDU guard under rstp — bpdu-block-on-edge applies to every edge port on the switch, so warn users translating from Cisco's per-interface bpduguard.

task: Show STP state for one port
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Front Panel > select port (port stats include STP role/state)
notes: Ruckus needs the VLAN because FastIron runs per-VLAN spanning tree, and the AOS-CX form assumes the default MSTP mode (instance 0 = CIST) — under RPVST+ use 'show spanning-tree detail' instead.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List (Power Draw column)
Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Switch Insights > Power Draw chart
notes: Cisco IOS has no separate budget command — available/used/remaining watts are the header lines of 'show power inline'.
Junos 'request interface bounce poe' requires Junos OS 24.2R1 or later; on older EX code you must 'set poe interface <port> disable', commit, then delete that statement and commit again.

task: Set PoE priority on a port

commands.cisco-ios:
interface {{interface}}
power inline port priority high

notes: Priority levels do not map 1:1 across vendors: Cisco IOS-XE (Catalyst 9K/3850-class only; older classic-IOS Catalysts have no priority command) and Junos offer only high/low with no critical, while Ruckus uses numeric 1=critical/highest through 3=low/default.

task: Show PoE detail for one port
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port List (per-port PoE draw)
notes: Ruckus takes the bare stack/slot/port here (show inline power 1/1/1) with no ethernet keyword, unlike its inline power config command.

commands.aos-cx:
interface lag 1
no shutdown
lacp mode active
interface {{interface}}
no shutdown
lag 1
commands.junos:
set chassis aggregated-devices ethernet device-count 2
set interfaces ae1 aggregated-ether-options lacp active
set interfaces {{interface}} ether-options 802.3ad ae1
commands.ruckus:
lag lag1 dynamic id 1
ports ethernet {{interface}}

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration — select the member ports and enable Port Aggregation (LACP).
notes: Junos only creates ae interfaces numbered up to device-count minus 1, so ae1 requires chassis aggregated-devices ethernet device-count of at least 2.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Front Panel — aggregated ports are highlighted; hover over or select a port to view LACP status.
notes: On AOS-S a "trunk" (Trk1) is a LAG, not an 802.1Q tagged port as in Cisco terminology, and VLAN membership is applied to Trk1 rather than the member ports.
Ruckus FastIron has no dedicated LACP neighbor command; the partner system ID and key appear in the "Partner Info and PDU Statistics" section of show lag output.

task: Show stack / VSF / Virtual Chassis members
mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] (Virtual Chassis panel shows members and roles)
notes: On AOS-S 'show vsf' applies to VSF-capable models like the 2930F; backplane-stacked models (2930M/3810M) use 'show stacking' instead, and the 2530 does not stack at all.
Cisco 'show switch stack-ports' is for StackWise stacks (9200/9300 class); StackWise Virtual pairs (9500/9600) use 'show stackwise-virtual link' instead.

task: Limit learned MACs on a port (port security)

commands.aos-cx:
port-access port-security enable
interface {{interface}}
port-access port-security enable
port-access port-security client-limit 2
commands.junos:
set switch-options interface {{interface}} interface-mac-limit 2
set switch-options interface {{interface}} interface-mac-limit packet-action drop
commands.cisco-ios:
interface {{interface}}
switchport mode access
switchport port-security
switchport port-security maximum 2
commands.ruckus:
interface ethernet {{interface}}
port security
enable
maximum 2

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Switches > [switch] > Port Configuration — set MAC Limit in the applied port profile
notes: AOS-CX needs the global 'port-access port-security enable' line as well as the interface ones, and Junos expects the logical unit in switch-options (ge-0/0/1.0, not ge-0/0/1).

task: Enable DHCP snooping on a VLAN

commands.cisco-ios:
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan {{vlan}}

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Organization > Switch Templates > [template] > DHCP Snooping
notes: Junos takes the VLAN name rather than the numeric ID, and on every vendor remember to mark DHCP-server-facing uplinks as trusted afterwards (Junos trusts trunk ports automatically).
On Ruckus the binding table comes from 'show ip dhcp snooping info' — plain 'show ip dhcp snooping' only shows feature status, not bindings.

mist_note: Mist dashboard: Clients > Wired Clients
notes: Aruba's 'show port-access clients' (both CX and AOS-S) lists MAC-auth and other authenticated clients alongside 802.1X — check the flags/auth-method column.

task: Show who is logged in to the switch
notes: On Ruckus 'show users' lists configured accounts rather than live sessions (use 'show who'), and AOS-S 'show telnet' covers console and SSH sessions despite its name.
